title: Purpose-limitation enforcement — processing not matching declared purpose
exclusions: Does NOT establish lawful basis itself; surfaces processing-vs-purpose mismatches for DPO review.
evidence: Saved search 'purpose_limitation_audit' (hourly) emits events where declared purpose in the access token disagrees with the processing system's declared purpose.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.5(1)(b) 'purpose limitation' requires technical enforcement wherever feasible; this UC detects observable mismatches.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.5(1)(b) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.37.4: Purpose-limitation enforcement — processing not matching declared purpose.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.37.4' running on Application audit logs carrying the purpose-bearing access token, system-purpose catalogue in purpose_catalogue.csv., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: CPRA §1798.100(b) requires use limitation to disclosed purposes; a mismatch is direct evidence of a deficiency.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CCPA §1798.100(b) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.37.4: Purpose-limitation enforcement — processing not matching declared purpose.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Derived from GDPR@2016/679 Art.5(1)(b) (parent assurance='partial'). UK GDPR preserves parent clause numbering; inherited assurance degraded one step.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that UK GDPR Art.5(1)(b) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.37.4: Purpose-limitation enforcement — processing not matching declared purpose.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.37.4' running on Application audit logs carrying the purpose-bearing access token, system-purpose catalogue in purpose_catalogue.csv., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A marketing job fetches data with token-purpose=personalization but reaches a system whose declared purpose is fraud-prevention only; the UC fires within the hour.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Token-purpose and system-purpose align; no alert.

dataSources: Application audit logs carrying the purpose-bearing access token, system-purpose catalogue in purpose_catalogue.csv.
spl:
index=app_activity earliest=-1h token_purpose=*
| lookup purpose_catalogue.csv system_id OUTPUT allowed_purposes
| eval ok=if(match(allowed_purposes,token_purpose),1,0)
| where ok=0
| stats count values(user) as users values(subject_token) as subjects by system_id token_purpose allowed_purposes
| table system_id token_purpose allowed_purposes count users subjects
description: Observes access tokens carrying a declared processing purpose and compares that purpose to the declared purposes of the system being accessed. Any mismatch is evidence that the purpose-limitation principle is not being enforced.
value: Converts an otherwise-abstract regulatory principle into a continuously-measurable control with evidentiary value.
implementation: (1) Mint OAuth/OIDC tokens with a purpose claim; (2) publish purpose_catalogue.csv keyed by system_id; (3) run hourly; (4) alert DPO on mismatches and drive remediation via SOAR.
visualization: Sankey of token-purpose → system, heatmap of mismatches by system, single-value 'mismatch events (24h)'.
knownFalsePositives: Shared service accounts without per-request purpose context cannot be evaluated; filter them out via service_account_allowlist.csv.
grandmaExplanation: Converts an otherwise-abstract regulatory principle into a continuously-measurable control with evidentiary value — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Application audit logs carrying the purpose-bearing access token, system-purpose catalogue in purpose_catalogue.csv..
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Mint OAuth/OIDC tokens with a purpose claim; (2) publish purpose_catalogue.csv keyed by system_id; (3) run hourly; (4) alert DPO on mismatches and drive remediation via SOAR.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=app_activity earliest=-1h token_purpose=*
| lookup purpose_catalogue.csv system_id OUTPUT allowed_purposes
| eval ok=if(match(allowed_purposes,token_purpose),1,0)
| where ok=0
| stats count values(user) as users values(subject_token) as subjects by system_id token_purpose allowed_purposes
| table system_id token_purpose allowed_purposes count users subjects
```

Understanding this SPL

**Purpose-limitation enforcement — processing not matching declared purpose** — Converts an otherwise-abstract regulatory principle into a continuously-measurable control with evidentiary value.

Documented **Data sources**: Application audit logs carrying the purpose-bearing access token, system-purpose catalogue in purpose_catalogue.csv. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: app_activity.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=app_activity, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **ok** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where ok=0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by system_id token_purpose allowed_purposes** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Pipeline stage (see **Purpose-limitation enforcement — processing not matching declared purpose**): table system_id token_purpose allowed_purposes count users subjects


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Sankey of token-purpose → system, heatmap of mismatches by system, single-value 'mismatch events (24h)'.
